rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE FRANCESCA P. NAIFY LIVING TRUST DATED NOVEMBER 30, 1990
rulings.Calendar Matter: Petition For Leave To File The Second Cause Of Action For Participation In Breach Of Trust Against John Sherwood, Individually
rulings.Rulings: (part 1 of 3) Petition for Leave to File Second Cause of Action in the Third Amended Petition is granted. This claim is not time-barred. To rule otherwise would render compliance with the Court of Appeal's order in Cortese v. Sherwood (2018) 26 Cal.App.5th 445 an exercise in futility. Civil Code § 1714.10(a) requires a substantially supported showing of a conspiracy claim. However, per subsection (c), subsection (a) "shall not apply" when either exception under (c) is pled. According to Berg & Berg Enterprises, LLC v. Sherwood Partners, Inc. (2005) 131 Cal. App. 4th 8020 818, "Since the statute now removes from its scope the two circumstances in which a valid attorney-client conspiracy claim may be asserted, its gatekeeping function applies only to attorney-client conspiracy claims that are not viable as a matter of law in any event. [citation.] Thus, a plaintiff who can plead a viable claim for conspiracy against an attorney need not follow the petition procedure outlined in the statute as such a claim necessarily falls within the stated exceptions to its application." The Berg court continued that the Court must initially determine whether the pleading falls within § 1714.10's coverage: "This determination pivots, in turn, on whether the proposed pleading states a viable claim for conspiracy against the attorney. [¿] If such a claim is stated, the analysis ends before reaching evidentiary considerations; the statute does not apply because the claim necessarily falls under one of the exceptions. If not stated, the analysis likewise ends, but with the opposite result; the pleading is disallowed for its failure to meet the initial gatekeeping hurdle of the statute." Berg concluded, "Both of [the exceptions under subsection (c)], by definition, involve conduct of the attorney in which he or she acts not just as an agent for a principal, the client, but also for himself or herself independently. In these settings then, it is appropriate that the agent's immunity rule does not protect the attorney's conduct, for he or she is acting in more than just a representative capacity. (Id.) Petitioner has sufficiently alleged facts that fall within § 1714.10(c)'s exceptions: (1) an independent legal duty owed to the beneficiary of a trust by the attorney for the trustee due to affirmative conduct by the attorney; and (2) acts beyond the performance of a professional duty to serve the client and involving a conspiracy to violate a legal duty in furtherance of financial gain.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE FRANCESCA P. NAIFY LIVING TRUST DATED NOVEMBER 30, 1990
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Petition For Leave To File The Second Cause Of Action For Participation In Breach Of Trust Against John Sherwood, Individually, Moving Party Chrisina Cortese (Added To Calendar For Tentative Ruling Entry Purposes Only *)
rulings.Rulings: (part 2 of 3) A. Breach of Duty In Morales v. Field, Degoff, Huppert & Macgowan (1979) 99 Cal. App. 3d 307, 315 and Berg & Berg Enterprises, LLC v. Sherwood Partners, Inc. (2005) 131 Cal. App. 4th 802, 832, the Court turned to Goodman v. Kennedy (1976) 18 Cal.3d 335, 342's multifactor balancing test to determine whether the trustee's attorney had breached a duty to the trust beneficiary with whom there was otherwise no privity: (1) the extent to which the transaction was intended to affect the plaintiff, (2) the foreseeability of harm to him, (3) the degree of certainty that the plaintiff suffered injury, (4) the closeness of the connection between the defendant's conduct and the injury suffered, (5) the moral blame attached to the defendant's conduct, and (6) the policy of preventing future harm. Petitioner alleges that Sherwood knowingly made false representations to her, and actively advised her regarding Francesca's Trust and estate so as to dissuade her from challenging Robert's management of the Trust and estate (Pet. 13, 54, 55, 56, 91(d)) and the Trust termination negotiation and his tax assessment (Pet. 12, 54, 55, 56, 82, 91(d), 93(e)); and that he did so to protect the conspiracy because Robert was giving him personal financial interests over and above his legal fees as compensation for participating in the conspiracy (Pet. 94-96). Petitioner alleges that Sherwood and Robert told her not to worry about Francesca's Trust being underfunded for tax purposes because she would inherit from Robert and be "a very wealthy woman," and that she relied on those assurances to her detriment. (Pet. 13, 15, 36, 37, 38, 48, 54, 55, 56, 59, 65, 91(d), 92). These allegations are similar to the allegations made in Morales v. Field, Degoff, Huppert & Macgowan (1979) 99 Cal.App.3d 307, 311-312, Wolf v. Mitchell, Silverberg & Knupp (1999) 76 Cal.App.4th 1030, 1040-1041, and Pierce v. Lyman (1991) 1 Cal.App.4th 1093, 1106 (and as discussed in Berg & Berg Enterprises, LLC v. Sherwood Partners, Inc. (2006) 131Cal.App.4th 802, 827-828). There, the courts found that the attorney for the trustee owed an independent duty to the trust beneficiaries due to affirmative conduct by the attorney that exceeded the capacity as agent for the trustee, either by committing actual fraud by advising them to take no action in order to conceal wrongful conduct by the Trustee, in Morales and Wolf, or by engaging in active concealment and misrepresentations in the pursuit of personal financial gain over and above their professional fees, as in Pierce.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE FRANCESCA P. NAIFY LIVING TRUST DATED NOVEMBER 30, 1990
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Petition For Leave To File The Second Cause Of Action For Participation In Breach Of Trust Against John Sherwood, Individually, Moving Party Christina Cortese (Added To Calendar For Tentative Ruling Entry Purposes Only **)
rulings.Rulings: (part 3 of 3) B. B. Financial Gain Petitioner alleges (at Pet. 14, 94, 95, 96) that Sherwood received investment opportunities before and after the alleged conspiracy began in 1997; that he received at least one bonus for a "special project" shortly after Francesca's allegedly underfunded estate had closed; a specific bequest in Robert's Trust; a lucrative position in Robert's company, and lucrative positions for his family members; and yearly monetary gifts beginning in 2009 (the year the termination agreement was signed mid-year). Petitioner also alleges that Sherwood intentionally filed false documents with the Court in connection with Francesca's estate (Pet. 91(b)), filed taxes reflecting the same incorrect information (Pet. 91(c)), and then gave Christina and Acela false Trust accountings, which did not reflect the Trust assets that should have been put into the Trust (Pet. 93(c)). Petitioner alleges that Sherwood did this in order to maintain access to investment opportunities, financial gifts from Robert, and lucrative employment for himself and his family members. As discussed in Pierce v. Lyman (1991) 1 Cal. App. 4th 1093, 1105, the attorney for the trustee was liable for the trustees' breached of duty because it was alleged that he "purposefully drafted and filed annual accountings with the probate court in order to conceal [the trustees'] breaches from the court. [The trustees' attorney] also used his authority over Trust assets and knowledge of Trust affairs to "gain access to investments on a preferential basis" and placed his own interest over the Trust interests. He concealed this self-dealing from the probate court." The Court of Appeal found these allegations qualified for the "personal financial advantage" requirement. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) and opposing parties no later than 4:00 p.m. Wednesday, February 6, 2019.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE FRANCESCA P. NAIFY LIVING TRUST DATED NOVEMBER 30, 1990
rulings.Calendar Matter: Petitioner Christina Corteses Notice Of Motion For Leave To File 3rd Amended Petition
rulings.Rulings: Motion for Leave to File Third Amended Petition is granted. There is no showing of unwarranted delay in bringing the new cause of action following discovery of new facts, or of prejudice to the opposing party in allowing this new cause of action to be pled. The proposed new Fourth Cause of Action relates back to the previous petitions, as recovery is sought on the same general set of facts. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) and opposing parties no later than 4:00 p.m. Wednesday, February 6, 2019.
